macro_segments.title: Whether Force of Circumstance (Ones) Affects the Validity of a Get with Conditions

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Question: Does Ones (Force of Circumstance) Invalidate a Conditional Get?
Proof from Mishna About Get Taking Effect After Death (Ein Get Leachar Misa)
Why the Mishna Teaches the Principle Twice: To Exclude Rabbi Yossi's Position on Dated Documents
The Mishna Case of 'If I Don't Return in Twelve Months' Shows Ones is Not a Factor
Distinction: Dying vs. Getting Sick as Ones - What the Person Would Have Intended

macro_segments.title: The Case of the River and Common vs. Uncommon Ones: Distinguishing Types of Force of Circumstance

micro_segments.title: The Man Who Tried to Return but Was Stopped by a River: Shmuel's Ruling That Ones Does Not Matter
Common vs. Uncommon Ones: Should the Person Have Anticipated It?
If Something Is Foreseeable, You Should Have Stipulated Otherwise

macro_segments.title: Raba's Position: Yesh Ones BeGittin - The Legal Principle vs. The Practical Policy

micro_segments.title: Raba's Own Reasoning (Sevara Dinafshei) for Why Ones Should Matter in Gets
The Policy Problem: Tznuos (Over-Cautious Women) and Prutzos (Over-Ready Women)
The Solution: Kol Demekadesh Adaata DeRabbanan - Retroactive Uprooting of Kiddushin

macro_segments.title: Hafka'as Kiddushin (Retroactive Uprooting of Marriage): The Mechanism and Its Limits

micro_segments.title: The Need for a 'Fig Leaf' Get: Why We Require a Defective Get to Apply Hafka'as Kiddushin
How Uprooting Works for Money-Based Kiddushin vs. Kiddushei Biah (Sexual Kiddushin)
Redefining Sexual Acts: Can Rabbis Retroactively Call Marital Relations 'Harlotry'?
Alternate View: Kiddushei Kesef is Only Derabbanan; Kiddushei Biah Never Recognized

macro_segments.title: The Other Position: Yesh Ones BeGittin - Ones Does Matter in Practice

micro_segments.title: Second View of Rava: Ones Does Matter Even in Practice, Not Just in Theory
Challenge from the Mishna: If Ones Mattered, Sickness Should Invalidate the Get
Response: Only Death (Not Sickness) Is Not Ones Because Death Was Anticipated
Conclusion: We Must Adopt the Position That Ain Ones BeGittin for Societal Stability

macro_segments.title: The Wednesday Wedding Takana (Ordinance): Its Original Rationale and Modern Application

micro_segments.title: Why Virgins Marry on Wednesday: Three Days to Prepare and Court Available on Thursday
The Takana Only Applies When Courts Are Fixed on Monday and Thursday
Hypothetical: If Courts Sat Every Day or Had No Fixed Schedule, Any Day Would Be Permitted
The Core Requirement: Three Days of Preparation, Not a Specific Day

macro_segments.title: The Source of the Takana: Rabbinical Solicitude for Daughters of Israel and the Three-Day Feast Preparation

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: Why Wednesday? The Rabbis Were Concerned (Shakdu) for Jewish Women's Welfare
The Pattern: Sunday, Monday, Tuesday Preparation; Wednesday Wedding (Three-Day Requirement)
Change in Practice: From Wednesday to Tuesday Due to Sakana (Danger)
Exception: Monday Permitted in Case of Ones (Special Circumstances)

macro_segments.title: The Nature of Sakana (Danger): Droit de Seigneur and Forced Sexual Relations

micro_segments.title: What Is the Sakana? The Danger of the Ruler Claiming First Right to the Bride
Why Call It Sakana Rather Than Ones? Because Some Women (Tznuot) Would Resist Unto Death
The Halacha: A Woman May Submit to Rape Rather Than Die; This Is Not Yehareg Ve'al Ya'avor
Why Not Educate Women? The Risk of False Claims and Misuse of This Halacha

macro_segments.title: Why the Takana Was Not Completely Uprooted: Long-Term Legal Systems Survive Temporary Threats

micro_segments.title: The Principle: Gezeirot (Edicts) Eventually Pass Away, But Takanot (Ordinances) Endure
Adaptation Without Uprooting: Shift to Tuesday Weddings Without Canceling the Original Takana
Why Tuesday Works: Uncertainty About the Wedding Day Prevents the Ruler from Intercepting
